meta.title: Mercedes CLC180 CLC200 Petrol Engine Replacement Cost UK
meta.description: Every Mercedes‑Benz CLC180 / CLC200 petrol engine — M271 1.8 Kompressor. CL203 generation, real UK costs and reliability. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

@graph.name: Mercedes‑Benz CLC180 / CLC200 Petrol Engine Era Map
Mercedes‑Benz CLC180 / CLC200 Petrol Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz CLC180 / CLC200 petrol engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M271 engine typically costs £1,200–£3,000 supply only for the CLC180, and £1,500–£4,000 for the CLC200. Fitting adds £700–£1,200. Timing chain replacement alone can cost £800–£2,500 if caught early. A replacement motor of type 271.957 costs on average £2,750. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz CLC180 / CLC200 petrol engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M271 is rated ★★½☆☆. It's a smooth, responsive engine, but it has a well‑documented reputation for premature timing chain and camshaft adjuster failure — the simplex chain can stretch, and the adjuster sprockets wear, potentially causing catastrophic engine failure. Other issues include vacuum leaks, oil leaks from camshaft position sensors, and failed thermostats.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz CLC180 / CLC200 petrol?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain and camshaft adjuster failure (the most critical — can destroy the engine), split vacuum hoses (causing rough idle), oil leaks from camshaft position sensors (can travel to the ECU), blocked crankcase breathers, failed thermostats, and occasional supercharger issues. The 5‑speed automatic transmission is bulletproof.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes‑Benz CLC180 / CLC200 petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and body condition. Against a well‑kept CLC worth £2,000–£5,000, a £2,200 recon is a significant investment — assess carefully. If the body is solid and the car has strong provenance, replacement can be worthwhile. Given the CLC's status as a stylish rear‑wheel‑drive coupe, good examples are worth saving.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz CLC petrol engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The CLC180 uses M271.946; the CLC200 uses M271.948 or M271.950. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M271's timing chain, or does it need a new engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A timing chain, camshaft adjuster and tensioner replacement (£800–£2,500) is far cheaper than a catastrophic chain failure (£1,200–£5,000 engine replacement). At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, book an inspection immediately. The early timing chain/sprocket wear can be assessed visually without a complete engine strip‑down.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz CLC180 / CLC200 Petrol Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a CLC petrol engine typically takes 8–12 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Timing chain replacement on the M271 is typically a one‑day job.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz CLC180 / CLC200 Petrol — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.problem: Timing chain and camshaft adjuster wear (M271)
rows.whenItMakesSense: If caught early; preemptive replacement of sprocket wheels is a plus

rows.problem: Oil leak from camshaft position sensors (M271)
rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catch it before oil reaches the ECU
Replacing the breather prevents engine management light issues
Replace when engine doesn't reach 80°C quickly
Repair or replace supercharger; less common than timing chain issues

buyingChecks.title: Buying This CLC Petrol? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with documented oil changes
* Evidence of timing chain and camshaft adjuster replacement — this is the number one preventative item
* No rattles on cold start — the single most important check
* 5‑speed automatic transmission (bulletproof) rather than the less popular manual
avoidIf: * Cold‑start rattle with no timing chain history — this is the single most important check
* Oil in the camshaft position sensor connectors — potential ECU damage risk
* Rough idle with no diagnosis — likely vacuum leak
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis documentation
* Poor service history — the M271 is unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The CLC180 and CLC200 are stylish, rear‑wheel‑drive coupes that offer a premium driving experience. But the M271 engine has a well‑deserved reputation for timing chain and camshaft adjuster failure — this can be catastrophic if ignored. Buy on service history and proof of preventative timing chain work. The 5‑speed automatic transmission is bulletproof. If you're replacing, a reconditioned unit with a 12‑month warranty is the sweet spot.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz CLC180 / CLC200 Petrol (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling noise on startup (timing chain and camshaft adjuster warning — the most critical check)
* Service History — Including which engine variant (M271.946 for CLC180, M271.948/950 for CLC200) — see the Era Map above
* Camshaft Sensor Connectors — Disconnect and check for oil — oil in the plug could mean a new ECU
* Idle Quality — Any hunting or rough idle? Vacuum hose split or crankcase breather blockage
* Engine Temperature — Does the engine reach 80°C quickly? Failed thermostat indicator
* Supercharger Health — Any unusual noise or loss of power? Supercharger issues are less common but possible
* Transmission — Check for leaking pilot bush on the 5‑speed automatic
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved timing, vacuum, or emissions issues?
